hook: You already know that two finite sets have the same size when you can pair up their elements with a perfect one-to-one correspondence. You've also seen that some infinite sets, like the natural numbers and the integers, can be matched bijectively. But now imagine you can build an injection from set $A$ into set $B$, and a separate injection from $B$ into $A$ — yet you cannot immediately see how to combine them into a single bijection. Can you always conclude that $|A| = |B|$?
concept: The Schröder-Bernstein Theorem says: yes, you can always draw that conclusion. Informally, if each set 'fits inside' the other, they must be the same size. Formally: if there exist injections $f: A \to B$ and $g: B \to A$, then there exists a bijection $h: A \to B$, and we write $|A| = |B|$.
anatomy: The theorem has four key structural pieces. First, the two injections: $f: A \to B$ (which embeds $A$ into $B$) and $g: B \to A$ (which embeds $B$ into $A$); neither needs to be surjective on its own. Second, the constructed bijection $h: A \to B$, which is built by partitioning $A$ into two disjoint classes. Third, the $A$-chain partition: for each $a \in A$, you trace the sequence $a, g^{-1}(a), f^{-1}(g^{-1}(a)), \ldots$ backwards through alternating applications of $g^{-1}$ and $f^{-1}$ until the chain either terminates in $A$, terminates in $B$, or loops infinitely. Fourth, the case-split definition of $h$: $h(a) = f(a)$ when the chain through $a$ terminates in $A$ or is infinite, and $h(a) = g^{-1}(a)$ when the chain terminates in $B$.

worked_example.description_latex: We want to show $|[0,1]| = |[0,1]|$ (trivially true, but let's rehearse the machinery) by building explicit injections. Define $f(x) = x/2 + 1/4$, which maps $[0,1]$ into $(1/4, 3/4) \subset [0,1]$. This is injective because it is strictly increasing, so $f(x_1) = f(x_2) \Rightarrow x_1 = x_2$.

Now define the second injection $g(y) = y/2$, mapping $[0,1]$ into $[0, 1/2] \subset [0,1]$. Again this is strictly increasing, hence injective. We now have $f: A \hookrightarrow B$ and $g: B \hookrightarrow A$ with $A = B = [0,1]$, exactly the hypothesis of Schröder-Bernstein.

To apply the theorem's construction, let $C_0$ be the set of elements of $A$ that are NOT in the image of $g$, meaning they have no $g$-preimage at all. Here $g(B) = [0, 1/2]$, so $C_0 = (1/2, 1]$. These elements can only be handled by $f$, because $g^{-1}$ does not exist for them — this is the starting seed of the chain construction.

We inductively define $C_n = g(f(C_{n-1}))$, propagating the 'tainted' elements through one round of $f$ then $g$. The union $C = \bigcup_{n \ge 0} C_n$ collects every element of $A$ whose backward chain eventually reaches $C_0$. All elements in $C$ will use $f$, while elements outside $C$ will use $g^{-1}$, and this split is precisely what prevents $h$ from clashing or missing anything.

worked_example.expression_latex: h(x) = \begin{cases} f(x) & x \in C \\ g^{-1}(x) & x \notin C \end{cases}
worked_example.description_latex: The bijection $h$ is defined piecewise: on $C$ we use $f$ (the safe direction for chain-terminators), and on $A \setminus C$ we use $g^{-1}$ (valid because every $x \notin C$ is guaranteed to lie in $g(B)$, making $g^{-1}(x)$ well-defined). The Schröder-Bernstein proof shows this $h$ is both injective and surjective, completing the argument that $|A| = |B|$.

partial_example.description_latex: We will prove this using Schröder-Bernstein by exhibiting two injections, one in each direction, rather than constructing a bijection directly. Start by identifying the two sets: $A = \mathbb{N} = \{0,1,2,\ldots\}$ and $B = \mathbb{Z}$.

The first injection $f$ simply includes $\mathbb{N}$ inside $\mathbb{Z}$ via the identity. This is clearly injective because $f(m) = f(n)$ implies $m = n$. We need you to supply the second injection going the other way.

Define an injection $g: \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{N}$. Your function must assign a distinct natural number to every integer (both positive and negative). Write down an explicit formula for $g(z)$ and verify it is injective.

Once both injections are verified, the Schröder-Bernstein Theorem immediately guarantees a bijection $h: \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Z}$ exists — you do not need to construct $h$ explicitly. The existence of the two injections is sufficient to conclude the sets have equal cardinality.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: State the Schröder-Bernstein Theorem: if $f: A \to B$ and $g: B \to A$ are both \underline{\hspace{1cm}}, then there exists a \underline{\hspace{1cm}} $h: A \to B$.
Use Schröder-Bernstein to show $|(0,1)| = |\mathbb{R}|$ by finding explicit injections $f:(0,1)\to\mathbb{R}$ and $g:\mathbb{R}\to(0,1)$.
practice_problems.answer_latex: One valid pair: $f(x)=x$ (inclusion), $g(t)=\tfrac{1}{1+e^{-t}}$ (the logistic function, which maps $\mathbb{R}$ into $(0,1)$). Both are injective, so Schröder-Bernstein gives $|(0,1)|=|\mathbb{R}|$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Show that $|\mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N}| = |\mathbb{N}|$ using Schröder-Bernstein by constructing two explicit injections.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Take $f: \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}\times\mathbb{N}$ by $f(n)=(n,0)$ (injective), and $g:\mathbb{N}\times\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ by $g(m,n)=2^m 3^n$ (injective by unique factorization). Schröder-Bernstein then gives $|\mathbb{N}\times\mathbb{N}|=|\mathbb{N}|$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Let $A = [0,1]$ and $B = [0,1)$. Use Schröder-Bernstein (not an explicit bijection) to prove $|A|=|B|$.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Define $f:A\to B$ by $f(x)=x/2$ (maps into $[0,1/2)\subset B$, injective) and $g:B\to A$ by $g(x)=x$ (inclusion, injective since $B\subset A$). Two injections exist, so Schröder-Bernstein gives $|[0,1]|=|[0,1)|$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A library has a catalogue $C$ of books and an archive $D$ of digital files. A librarian shows there is an injective tagging $f:C\to D$ and an injective scan $g:D\to C$. A new librarian claims this is not enough to guarantee a perfect pairing between books and files — they insist an explicit bijection must be exhibited. Is the new librarian correct? Justify with the relevant theorem.
practice_problems.answer_latex: No, the new librarian is incorrect. By the Schröder-Bernstein Theorem, the existence of injections $f:C\to D$ and $g:D\to C$ is sufficient to guarantee $|C|=|D|$, meaning a bijection must exist, even if it is not explicitly constructed.

common_mistakes: Students assume the two injections $f$ and $g$ must be inverses of each other, writing $g = f^{-1}$. This is wrong: $f$ and $g$ are completely independent functions; neither needs to be surjective, and they can be defined by entirely different formulas.

Students try to 'compose' $f$ and $g$ directly to get the bijection $h = f \circ g^{-1}$, forgetting that $g^{-1}$ may not be defined on all of $A$ (since $g$ need not be surjective). The correct construction requires the chain-partition of $A$ into $C$ and $A\setminus C$ before deciding which function to use at each point.

Students conclude that because Schröder-Bernstein guarantees a bijection exists, any particular function they can write down (such as $f$ itself) must already be that bijection. In reality, $f$ is only an injection; the bijection $h$ is a new, explicitly constructed function, and in many cases $f$ is not surjective and therefore cannot serve as $h$.
untested_variants: Applying Schröder-Bernstein to compare the cardinality of the power set $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$ with the set of real numbers $\mathbb{R}$, requiring injections involving binary representations or Cantor's diagonal argument.

Using Schröder-Bernstein in the context of ordinal or cardinal arithmetic to show that $\aleph_0 + \aleph_0 = \aleph_0$ or $\aleph_0 \cdot \aleph_0 = \aleph_0$ by constructing the relevant injections formally.

Proving that Schröder-Bernstein fails for surjections — that is, constructing an example where surjections $f: A \twoheadrightarrow B$ and $g: B \twoheadrightarrow A$ exist but $|A| \ne |B|$ is impossible (and explaining why the theorem's hypotheses are specifically about injections).

Walking through the full formal proof of the Schröder-Bernstein Theorem, including verifying that the piecewise-defined $h$ is both injective and surjective via the chain-decomposition argument, rather than merely applying the theorem as a black box.
